content.problemStatement:
Chef has a 222-D grid with NNN rows and MMM columns. The cell at the intersection of the iii-th row and jjj-th column is denoted cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j).

Initially, only cell (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) of the grid is infected.

Chef can select **exactly one** non-infected cell and vaccinate it.
Then, the infection and the vaccine spread across the grid, as follows:


First, any cell that is neither vaccinated nor infected, but is adjacent (horizontally or vertically) to an infected cell, becomes infected itself.
Next, any cell that is neither vaccinated nor infected, but is adjacent (horizontally or vertically) to a vaccinated cell, becomes vaccinated itself.
This process repeats till every cell in the grid is either infected or vaccinated.

For example, on a 6×66\times 66×6 grid with (x,y)=(2,2)(x, y) = (2, 2)(x,y)=(2,2) and the vaccine placed at (5,4)(5, 4)(5,4) initially, the spread would look like:



Find the **maximum** possible number of vaccinated cells Chef can obtain, if he chooses the initial position of the vaccine optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the dimensions of the grid.
The second line of each test case contains two space-separated integers xxx and yyy — the coordinates of the infected cell.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum number of vaccinated cells attainable.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It's optimal to vaccinate cell (2,2)(2, 2)(2,2).
The spread of infection and vaccine is visualized below.

666 cells are vaccinated, and this is the best we can do.
Test case 222: It's optimal to vaccinate cell (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1).
The spread of infection and vaccine is visualized below.

444 cells are vaccinated, which is the best we can do.
